title: Sliding Window in Java
summary: Fixed-size and variable-size window patterns, max/min subarray, longest substring with constraints, and the shrink-when-invalid template.

questions.prompt: Sliding window improves nested-loop O(n²) to O(n) because:
choices: Each element enters and leaves the window at most once — l and r only move right
It uses extra O(n) space always
questions.explanation: l only increments, r only increments — total moves across both pointers ≤ 2n = O(n).

questions.prompt: Fixed-size window: to evict the oldest element when window size exceeds k, remove:
choices: nums[r - k]  (element that is now k positions behind r)
questions.explanation: When r=k, the element that fell out is nums[r-k] = nums[0]. Pattern: windowSum -= nums[r-k].

questions.prompt: Max sum of window k=3 on [2,1,5,1,3,2]:
questions.explanation: Window [5,1,3] gives sum 9 — the maximum.

questions.prompt: Variable window: the window shrinks when:
choices: The window state violates the problem constraint
The window is larger than k
questions.explanation: Shrink left until the window becomes valid again — then expand right and repeat.

questions.prompt: Longest substring without repeating characters — window state is:
choices: Frequency map of characters in the window
questions.explanation: A character is 'repeating' when its frequency in the window exceeds 1.

questions.prompt: Longest substring without repeats in 'abcabcbb':
questions.code:
// windows: abc(3), bca(3), cab(3), abc(3), bcb(3)→shrink, cb(2), b(1)
// max length:
questions.explanation: The longest window without a repeat is 'abc' = length 3.

questions.prompt: Window size formula using left and right pointers:
questions.explanation: A window from index l to r (inclusive) contains r - l + 1 elements.

questions.prompt: When minimising window size (e.g. minimum window substring), update the answer:
choices: Inside the shrink loop — each valid window that fits is a candidate minimum
questions.explanation: Every time the window is valid (all required chars present), check if it is smaller than the current best, then try shrinking more.

questions.prompt: When maximising window size, update the answer:
choices: After the shrink loop — when the window is as large as it can be while still valid
questions.explanation: After shrinking to restore validity, the window is the largest valid window ending at r.

questions.prompt: Maximum number of vowels in a substring of length k uses:
choices: Fixed window of exactly size k
questions.explanation: Window size is fixed at k — slide it and count vowels entering/leaving.

questions.prompt: Minimum size subarray sum >= target variable window expands right until sum >= target, then:
choices: Records length and shrinks left to find a smaller valid window
questions.explanation: While sum >= target: record current window length as candidate minimum, then remove left element.

questions.prompt: Min subarray length with sum >= 7 on [2,3,1,2,4,3]:
questions.explanation: Subarray [4,3] sums to 7 with length 2 — the minimum.

questions.prompt: Sliding window maximum (max of every k-width window) optimal solution:
choices: Fixed window O(n) with prefix max
Monotonic deque O(n) — front always holds max of current window
questions.explanation: Deque stores candidate indices in decreasing value order; front = current window max. Each element pushed/popped once = O(n).

questions.prompt: Fruit Into Baskets (at most 2 distinct fruit types, max fruits collected) maps to:
choices: Variable window: longest subarray with at most 2 distinct values
questions.explanation: State = frequency map of fruits in window. Shrink when distinct count > 2.

questions.prompt: Minimum window substring 'satisfied' counter tracks:
choices: How many characters of t are in the window
How many distinct characters of t have their required frequency met
Number of chars in s scanned
questions.explanation: satisfied increments only when have[c] exactly reaches need[c] — not on every occurrence.

questions.prompt: Which problems are solved with a variable sliding window?
choices: Longest substring with at most k distinct chars
Maximum sum subarray of fixed size k
Minimum window containing all target chars
Longest subarray with sum at most S
questions.explanation: Fixed-size max sum uses fixed window. The other three have a variable constraint requiring dynamic resizing.

questions.prompt: Character frequency tracking inside a window uses int[128] (ASCII) instead of HashMap when:
choices: Character set is limited to ASCII — array gives O(1) access with less overhead
questions.explanation: For known bounded character sets (ASCII, lowercase letters), int[] is faster and simpler than HashMap.

questions.prompt: After adding nums[r] to the window and shrinking (if needed), window [l, r] is guaranteed:
choices: The largest valid window ending at index r
questions.explanation: l was moved just enough to restore validity — making [l,r] the biggest valid window with right boundary r.

questions.prompt: Longest subarray with sum <= 5 on [3,1,2,4,1]:
questions.explanation: Maximum valid window length seen is 2 (e.g. [3,1] or [1,4] after shrink or [4,1]).

questions.prompt: Sliding window is NOT the right technique when:
choices: You need a non-contiguous subsequence or the subproblem structure is not monotonic
Window state is a running sum
questions.explanation: Sliding window requires: contiguous window AND monotonic validity (adding elements only makes constraint harder/easier, not arbitrary). Non-contiguous problems use DP.

flashcards.back: Add nums[r]. If r >= k: remove nums[r-k]. If r >= k-1: update answer.
Expand r. While invalid: shrink l. Update answer after while loop.
r - l + 1 elements when window spans indices [l, r] inclusive.
l and r each move right at most n times — total work is O(n).
Maximise: update after shrink. Minimise: update INSIDE while(valid) then shrink.
Running sum (int), frequency map (int[128] or HashMap), distinct count (int).
Monotonic decreasing deque of indices. Front = current window maximum.
Sliding window works when adding elements monotonically tightens/loosens constraint.

project.brief:
Write SlidingWindowLab.java with:
1. maxSumFixed(int[] nums, int k) → int
2. lengthOfLongestSubstringKDistinct(String s, int k) → int
3. minSizeSubarraySum(int[] nums, int target) → int  (return 0 if none)
4. maxSlidingWindow(int[] nums, int k) → int[]  (monotonic deque)
5. minWindowSubstring(String s, String t) → String
Include main() tests: k=1, k=n, target larger than total sum, t longer than s.

checklist.text: lengthOfLongestSubstringKDistinct shrinks until distinct count <= k
minSizeSubarraySum returns 0 when no subarray meets target
maxSlidingWindow evicts front when index out of window, back when value smaller than new element
minWindowSubstring uses satisfied counter — only increments when frequency exactly meets requirement

hints: maxSlidingWindow: evict dq.peekFirst() when dq.peekFirst() < r - k + 1.
minWindowSubstring: have[c]-- then check if have[c] < need[c] AFTER the decrement before deciding to decrement satisfied.
lengthOfLongestSubstringKDistinct: remove from map when count reaches 0 to keep map.size() == distinct count.
